prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 34-year-old man.
I looked up abnormal thyroid tests online and now I'm terrified this could be thyroid cancer.

My situation:
- Symptoms: Over the past few months I've felt more tired than usual and a bit more sensitive to cold. No chest pain or shortness of breath.
- History: No known thyroid disease before.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual thyroid lab results, so I can’t interpret what’s “abnormal” or estimate cancer risk from numbers. Based only on your symptoms (fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months) and no red-flag symptoms like chest pain or shortness of breath, this pattern is more consistent with a non-emergency issue such as an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer more often presents with a new neck lump, enlarging thyroid, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck nodes—none of which you mentioned. Still, persistent symptoms for months warrant a routine medical visit to review labs, repeat tests if needed, and check your neck.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include any actual thyroid lab results, so it isn’t possible to interpret “abnormal thyroid tests” or estimate cancer risk from this message alone. Your symptoms (fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months) are more consistent with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer most often presents as a new neck lump/swelling, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or enlarged neck lymph nodes—not just tiredness/cold intolerance. Even if thyroid blood tests are abnormal, they usually reflect hormone imbalance (common and treatable) rather than cancer. The next step is to review the exact test values (TSH, free T4, possibly T3 and thyroid antibodies) with a clinician and decide if repeat testing, treatment, or an exam/ultrasound is needed.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include any actual thyroid lab results or a neck lump/hoarseness/trouble swallowing, so I can’t interpret “abnormal thyroid tests” specifically. Based only on your symptoms (fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months), the most common thyroid-related explanation would be an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism), which is usually not an emergency and is much more common than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer typically presents as a thyroid nodule/lump or enlarged neck nodes and often doesn’t cause hypothyroid symptoms. The right next step is to review the exact test values (TSH, free T4, possibly thyroid antibodies) with a clinician and decide if repeat testing, treatment, or imaging is needed.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include any actual thyroid lab results or findings (like a neck lump, trouble swallowing, hoarseness, or abnormal imaging). Based only on what you shared, the symptoms (fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months) fit more with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer often presents as a new thyroid nodule or neck mass and many people have no symptoms at all; blood tests alone usually don’t diagnose it. The right next step is a routine medical visit to review symptoms, do an exam of your neck, and check thyroid labs (typically TSH and free T4) and other common causes of fatigue.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual thyroid lab results, so I can’t interpret what was “abnormal” or estimate cancer risk from numbers. Based only on what you shared—months of fatigue and increased cold sensitivity with no chest pain or shortness of breath—this pattern can fit an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism), which is common and usually not cancer. Thyroid cancer more often presents with a new neck lump, enlarging thyroid, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck nodes; you didn’t report these. The appropriate next step is a non-urgent appointment with your doctor to review the exact tests (typically TSH and free T4), repeat/confirm if needed, and check for common causes (e.g., autoimmune thyroiditis) and treatment.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual thyroid test numbers, so I can’t interpret “abnormal results.” Based only on your symptoms (fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months) and no urgent symptoms like chest pain or shortness of breath, this pattern is more consistent with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer usually presents with a neck lump, enlarging thyroid nodule, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck lymph nodes; abnormal routine thyroid blood tests alone don’t typically indicate cancer. The appropriate next step is to review the exact lab values (TSH, free T4, possibly thyroid antibodies) with your doctor to confirm the cause and decide if treatment is needed.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include any actual thyroid lab results, so I can’t interpret “abnormal thyroid tests” or estimate cancer risk from numbers. Based only on what you shared, your symptoms (fatigue and feeling cold) fit more with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer often presents as a new neck lump, enlarging thyroid nodule, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck lymph nodes—none of which you mentioned. Hypothyroidism is common and usually not an emergency, but it should be evaluated with a clinician and repeat/confirmatory labs.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual thyroid lab values or any exam/imaging findings, so it isn’t possible to interpret “results” or estimate cancer risk from this message alone. Your symptoms (fatigue and feeling cold) are more commonly associated with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer often presents as a new neck lump, enlarging thyroid nodule, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck lymph nodes—none of which you mentioned. Most abnormal thyroid blood tests reflect hormone imbalance (often treatable) rather than cancer. The appropriate next step is to review the specific test numbers (TSH, free T4, possibly T3, thyroid antibodies) with your doctor and decide whether repeat testing, medication, or (if a lump is present) an ultrasound is needed.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You mention fatigue and increased cold sensitivity over months with no prior thyroid disease, but you didn’t include any actual thyroid lab results or findings (like a neck lump). Those symptoms can fit an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism), which is common and usually not an emergency. Thyroid cancer typically presents with a thyroid nodule/lump, neck swelling, voice changes, or trouble swallowing/breathing; abnormal blood tests alone don’t usually indicate cancer. Because your symptoms have persisted for months and could reflect a treatable thyroid hormone problem (or other causes like anemia, sleep issues, etc.), it’s reasonable to arrange a routine medical visit to review symptoms and get/interpret labs.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (fatigue and feeling cold) fit more with an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism) than with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer usually presents as a new neck lump, enlarging thyroid nodule, hoarseness, trouble swallowing, or swollen neck nodes; it often does not cause cold intolerance. “Abnormal thyroid tests” can happen for many non-cancer reasons (temporary illness effects, medication/supplement effects, autoimmune thyroiditis, iodine issues), and most thyroid test abnormalities are not emergencies. The appropriate next step is a routine medical visit to review the exact lab values, repeat/confirm tests if needed, and decide whether further evaluation (like thyroid antibodies or ultrasound) is warranted.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 72%
